topic: A person finds out their biography was written without their knowledge
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias traced the embossed lettering on the aged parchment, the ink a deep, unsettling black against the cream paper. Every sentence was a carefully constructed edifice of his life, a narrative he’d only ever glimpsed in fragmented memories. He leaned back in the worn leather armchair, the springs groaning in protest beneath him. His fingers drummed a quick, uneven rhythm on his knee. It wasn't just the content—the dates, the career milestones, the whispered anecdotes about his childhood—it was the sheer, audacious violation of his reality. A cold prickle crawled up his spine, making the fine hairs on his arms stand on end. He pushed the document away, the sound sharp in the quiet study. He needed movement. He stood abruptly, pacing the length of the room, his gaze darting from the window to the shadowed corners of the library. The air felt too thick, too stagnant. He needed air, needed noise, needed anything to break this suffocating stillness.

[story 2]

Clara kept turning the glossy pages over and over, her jaw tight enough to ache. The biography detailed a life she barely recognized—a trajectory mapped out by someone else’s hand, complete with invented motivations and fabricated triumphs. Her hands began to tremble, a slight vibration that she tried to suppress by gripping the edge of the desk until her knuckles bleached white. She rose from the sofa, the movement jerky and uncoordinated. She walked to the balcony, the cool night air offering no solace, only a sharp contrast to the heat building behind her ribs. She stared out at the city lights, each distant gleam seeming to mock the curated perfection of the text she held. A deep, insistent thrumming started in her chest, demanding an outlet. She turned back inside, her steps quickening, needing to do something, anything, to dispel the heavy pressure building inside her.

[story 3]

Marcus kept running his thumb over the signature line, a tremor subtly running through his hand. The account was flawless, intimate in a way that felt utterly alien. He felt a profound internal churning, an urgent need to dismantle the structure, to pull the pieces apart until the scaffolding collapsed. He paced the narrow hallway, his boots clicking a frantic, uneven rhythm against the hardwood floor. Every corner he rounded brought a fresh wave of disorientation. He stopped by the mirror, studying his own face—a stranger wearing his features—and a tight, almost painful smile touched his lips before vanishing. He needed to change the environment, to inject some chaotic energy into the vacuum the discovery had created. He grabbed his coat, the fabric feeling suddenly too restrictive, and headed out into the damp evening, needing the friction of the outside world.
